Truckeeâ€™s Patriarch, Historian, Author, Editor, Attorney, Legislator, Inventor, Entomologist and Astronomer. His last public address was given in 1926 at the Donner Summit Bridge Dedication. This site is named in his memory by the people of Truckee.

properties.name: Memorial Grove Nevada City
properties.description: This grove honors those who served our country in the armed forces. It contains five plaques. Plaque dedicated 1946 by the citizens of Nevada City: This plot of land set aside by the City of Nevada, these trees living tributes to our war dead. This plaque and flag pole erected by the citizens of Nevada City dedicated July 4, 1946 to the memory of our heroes who gave their lives in World War II that the American way of life may continue. Caswell Fisher, Tevis Ferguson, Merton Downey, Donald Osrey, Bert Graham, Fred Doby, Eugene Davies, Albert Conti, Ernest Chapman, William G. Bennett, Hans G. Hirsef, Clifford M. Ruth, Timothy R. Livesay, Virgil Roseborough, David McCreary, Eugene Rickard, Robert D. Proctor, Louis G. Netz, Glenn Ponticelli , Walter O. St. John, Carl D. Tobiassen, Gus Wasley, James M. Odegaard, William Pohley, Reid Shurtleff. Plaque dedicated 1990 by Banner Mountain Post No. 2655, Veterans of Foreign Wars: Korea, June 25, 1950 - July 27, 1953, 'The Forgotten War - Remembered.' Let us not forget those Americans who served, those who died and those who still remain on that foreign soil in defense of freedom. As a result of that war: 54,246 Americans were killed 8,177 Americans are missing in action 389 Americans are prisoners of war. Plaque dedicated 1992 by Banner Mountain Post No. 2655, Veterans of Foreign Wars: Vietnam. All gave some - some gave all. If you are able, save for them a place inside of you and save one, backward glance when you are leaving for the places they can no longer, go. Be not ashamed to say you loved them, though you may or may not, have always. Take what they have left and what they have taught you, with their dying and keep it with your own. And in that time when men, decide and feel safe to call the war insane, take one moment to, embrace those gentle heroes you left behind. - Major Michael Davis O'Donnell, 1 January 1970, Dak To, Vietnam. Note: Major O'Donnell was a helicopter pilot. On 24 March 1970, while attempting to rescue eight soldiers, his chopper was shot down. He and, three crew members were declared missing in action. This memorial plaque is dedicated as a tribute to those who served and fought in the Vietnam War, especially those who died, and those yet to return. Plaque dedicated 1995 by Banner Mountain Post No. 2655, Veterans of Foreign Wars: POW MIA You are not forgotten. Plaque dedicated 2007 by the citizens of Nevada County: War on Terror We Will not Forget. In honor of the Men and Women who have served and will serve in the ongoing Global War on Terror and to those Valiant Few who have given their lives, therein... . We, the citizens of Nevada County, dedicate this memorial. L/Cpl Adam Strain, 20, L/Cpl John 'J.T.' Lucente, 19, 1st Lt. Nathan Krisoff, 25, Cpl Sean Stokes, 24, Ssg David P. Senft, 27. There are also memorial plaques for the following Vietnam veterans located on the following Golden Center Freeway overpasses around Nevada City. PfcUSA: Philip A. Tritsch 06/10/1943-01/28/1969, west end Washington St SP/4 101st ABN: Ronald J. Walber 06/3/1948-04/25/1968, west end Broad St Michael D. Goeller 06/2/1948-06/01/1969, east end Sacramento St Capt USA: John Stuart Seeley 04/10/1932-06/27/1966, west end Gold Flat Rd 1st Lt USA: James F

properties.name: Memorial Park GV
properties.address: Memorial Park, corner of Race St and Colfax Ave, Grass Valley
properties.description: Memorial Park was built in 1921 in memory of local armed forces members who died in World War I. The land and swimming pool were donated by the Empire Mine Co.; the club house was donated by the Idaho Maryland Mine Co.; and people of the area subscribed $8,000. It now contains five plaques honoring those who served our country in the armed forces.

properties.name: Mooney Flat Hotel
properties.address: In front of private home at 10885 Mooney Flat Rd, one mile north of Hwy 20
properties.description: Mooney Flat Hotel 1856-1958. Site of the first 3-story team & stage stop on the Henness Pass route to the Northern Mines and Comstock Lode. Built and operated by George and Veronica Schmidt. Served miners, travelers, and teamsters.

properties.name: Mt. Elisha Stephens
properties.address: Parking lot next to Donner Summit Bridge on Donner Pass Rd, Old Hwy 40
properties.description: Stephens-Townsend-Murphy Party of 1844 First Pioneer Wagons Over the Sierra Nevada. On May 22, 1844, this small wagon party of 50 men, women and children 'jumped off' from Council Bluffs, Iowa, bound for California. These courageous pioneers were the first emigrants to take wagons over the Sierra Nevada, opening the Truckee Route of the California Trail. The Murphy, Townsend, Miller, and Hitchcock families formed the main groups of the party. Caleb Greenwood was the hired guide, and Elisha Stephens was elected Captain of the wagon train. After following the Oregon Trail to Fort Hall, Idaho, they turned away from the well-worn path and headed west into the unfamiliar Nevada desert. A Paiute Indian Chief showed them how to reach a river flowing from the Sierra Nevada, which they named 'Truckee' in his honor. Overcoming severe conditions, they followed the Truckee River to the base of the Sierra. Six members, on horseback, continued to follow the Truckee River and eventually arrived safely at Sutter's New Helvetia on December 10, 1844. Six of the wagons were left in the charge of two men, and young Moses Schallenberger, near Truckee Lake (present Donner Lake). Near this spot, with two feet of snow on the ground, they worked the remaining five wagons across the summit of the mighty Sierra Nevada on November 25, 1844. Deepening snow forced them to camp near Big Bend, on the South Yuba River. Leaving the wagons with two men and the women and children, the remaining 17 men continued on to New Helvetia to seek help. Somehow, John Sutter persuaded the men to enlist in his forces taking part in a Mexican civil war. Eventually the families were reunited, and all members (including two babies born on the way) arrived safely at Sutterâ€™s Fort in early March 1845. Note: Mount Elisha Stephens is west of bridge on north side of road.

properties.name: Mt. Saint Mary's Convent and Academy
properties.description: Mt. St. Mary's School. To this spot Father Thomas J. Dalton brought the Sisters of Mercy in 1863. The Sisters of Mercy have taught here since that date. Landmark registered and second plaque dedicated 1972 Built by the Reverend Thomas J. Dalton and dedicated May 2, 1865 by Bishop Eugene O'Connell as Sacred Heart Convent and Holy Angels Orphanage. Under the Sisters of Mercy it served from 1866 to 1932 as the first orphanage of the Northern Mines. It functioned as an academy (1868-1965) and a convent (1866-1968).

properties.name: Murphy Cabin
properties.address: In Donner Memorial State Park, a few hundred yds south of Museum on trail
properties.description: The face of this rock formed the north end and the fireplace of the Murphy Cabin. General Stephen W. Kearney, on June 22, 1847, buried under the middle of the cabin the bodies found in the vicinity. Following is a complete list of members of the Donner party who occupied the various cabins and tents.This and the other Donner campsites were designated a National Historical Landmark in 1961. Placed on the National Register.

properties.name: Naffziger House
properties.description: Boyhood Home of Howard Christian Naffziger. 1884 M.D., F.A.C.S. 1961. World-renowned neurosurgeon. Professor of surgery, Chief of Surgery and Neurosurgery at University of California Medical School; and Regent of the University of California. House built in 1859 by J. H. Helms. Note: James Harvey Helm (not "Helms") was a City Trustee and operated the Empire Livery Stable on Broad Street.

properties.name: Nathaniel P. Brown House
properties.description: Nathaniel P. Brown, founding owner of the Nevada Daily Transcript and early political figure in Nevada City, lived here from 1865 until 1909. He expanded the original cottage to its current size in the 1880s, allowing for frequent entertaining. The home was built in 1858 by Jesse S. Wall, an early settler and merchant. Nathaniel Brown came to Nevada City from Massachusetts in 1853 at the age of 18, following his friend Aaron Sargent. He was part owner of the weekly Nevada Journal from 1855 to 1859. In 1860, Brown founded the countyÃ

properties.name: National Exchange Hotel
properties.description: Originally the Bicknell Block, stagecoach, mail, express and telegraph center in mining days. Companies which became the Pacific Gas & Electric formed here. Site of first whipping post in state. Facade unchanged since 1854. This hotel, one of the oldest in continuous operation west of the Rockies, opened for business in August, 1856. Originally known as the 'Bicknell Block,' the structure consists of three common-walled buildings of simple brick construction embellished with classic revival and wrought iron railing ornamentation.

properties.name: Nevada Brewery
properties.description: The Old Brewery. Constructed of granite blocks from the Sierras about 1882. Where Simon C. Hieronimus & family brewed and served lager beer to Nevada City, Queen of the Northern Mines and hydraulic mining communities. Note: This brewery was erected on the site of Temperance Hall after that building burned. After his brewery on Searls Avenue (See 102) burned in 1893, Simon Hieronimus brewed his beer in this building until he died in 1911.

properties.name: Nevada City Downtown Historic DIstrict
properties.description: Nevada City, the 'Queen City of the Northern Mines', became a town of 10,000 to 16,000 in the early 1850s following the discovery of gold on Deer Creek in 1849. Here is located the largest and best preserved historical downtown district in California Gold Country. As the county seat of Nevada County, Nevada City has maintained its position as the countyÃ

properties.name: Nevada City Firehouse No. 1
properties.description: Nevada Hose Co. No. 1 Occupied this firehouse from May 30, 1861 to 1938. Hand pulled hose carts, horse drawn engines and motorized pumpers all rolled out of this building to save life and property. Note: The building now houses the Historical Society Museum.

properties.name: Nevada City Firehouse No. 2
properties.description: Firehouse #2 or Pennsylvania Engine Company #2 January 14,1861. Fire company formed June 13,1860 as Eureka Hose No. 2 with 34 volunteers. Name changed in August of that year to 'Pennsylvania Engine.' Construction money raised in 1859 by popular subscription and a grand ball staged by the townÃ

properties.name: Nevada City Hall
properties.description: First plaque dedicated 1937 by Native Sons of the Golden West: Dedicated June 19,1937 to the Pioneer Councilmen who erected the first City Hall Felix Gillet, K. Casper, J. C. Locklin, Alec. Gault, Dan. Baker and to H. D. Shearer and Wm. H. Kilroy Officers who were killed in line of duty.... Second plaque (on floor of porch): This building dedicated to Truth-Liberty-Toleration by the Native Sons of the Golden West June 19,1937.Third plaque dedicated 1987 by Native Sons of the Golden West: U. S. Constitution. 1787 Bicentennial Nevada City. A Bicentennial Community. Fourth plaque dedicated 2000 by Nevada City Council: On February 14, 2000 a contract was executed, authorizing the completion of work that began in 1937. On that date, the City Council inaugurated a process by which a small, historically significant City Hall was transformed into a spacious and efficient public facility. Expansion and renovation have been fruitful to the original architectural style and designed to serve the community far in to the 21st century.... Dedicated to all who came before us and all who will follow. Fifth plaque dedicated 2000 by Nevada City Council: On the occasion of the dedication of a new City Hall at this location on December 19, 2000, the citizens and City Council of Nevada City humbly submit for posterity a time capsule secured in a concrete vault beneath this marker. Contained within are artifacts and documents representative of life in Nevada City as we prepare to embrace the uncharted future. We respectfully request this repository remain undisturbed until April 17, 2150, at which time Nevada City will celebrate its 300th birthday.

properties.name: Nevada City High School Site
properties.address: Near sidewalk, across from 626 Zion St
properties.description: Plaque dedicated by former students: Nevada City High School was located on this site from 1912-1952. This plaque was placed by former students as a lasting memorial to their high school. Note: After merger of Grass Valley and Nevada City high schools, Seven Hills School used the building as a junior high school. In 1973 the buildings were sold to the County of Nevada and eventually torn down. A charter school occupies the adjoining grounds.

properties.name: Nevada City Jewish Cemetery
properties.address: End of King Hiram Lane, off Searls Ave
properties.description: The Nevada City Jewish Cemetery was established in 1854. The first burial was in 1856 and the last in 1890. Twenty nine headstones are visible, including that of Louis W. Dreyfuss, a cousin of Captain Alfred Dreyfus of France, the victim in the infamous Dreyfus Affair. The cemetery was restored around 50 years ago by the Commission for the Preservation of Pioneer Jewish Cemeteries and Landmarks. It is locked and fenced to protect against vandalism. Note: For more information see A Traveler's Guide to Pioneer Jewish Cemeteries of the California Gold Rush by Susan Morris.

properties.name: Nevada City Masonic Lodge
properties.description: The year was 1850 at a location not far from here. The first Masonic Lodge meeting was held under a Charter from state of Wisconsin. Lafayette Lodge #29 was born, the years following in 1851 & 1863 a major part of which was to be known as Nevada City was destroyed by fires. By this time California was a State and a new Lodge of California Charter was established as Nevada #13 F&AM in 1851. At this location the first masonry structure was built in 1852 as a store. The 1863 fire destroyed this building and again the Lodge at a different place. This site was purchased for $1,650 and rebuilt as a store with Lodge rooms up stairs. The Lodge moved in 1864. A dining room/office extension was built to the right in 1901. Over the past 136 years other Masonic units/orders have also been meeting here. Royal Arch, Nevada Chapter #6, Council #62, K. T Commandery #6, Evangeline Eastern Star #9. In honor of 150 years continuous Nevada Lodge #13 F&AM Masonic Actives. Grand Master of California Alvin J. Weis placed this plaque in rededication of this Lodge April 22, 2000. Note: The plaque is quoted verbatim; all errors are in the original.

properties.name: Nevada City Methodist Church
properties.description: Historic Nevada City Methodist Church. Dedicated in 1864. Now one of the oldest existing churches.

properties.name: Nevada County Court House
properties.address: 201 Church St, on left side of entrance
properties.description: Nevada County Courthouse History. Nevada County organized out of Yuba County May 11,1851. Wooden court-house and log jail built on Broad Street in 1851. The first courthouse on this site was erected in 1856. The building was damaged by fire in July of the same year and rebuilt. Destroyed by fire in 1863 and rebuilt in 1864. Remodeled and enlarged in 1900. Modernized and enlarged 1936-37, George C. Sellon, Architect, Lindgren and Swinerton, Inc., Contractors, Robert Steger, Building Superintendent.

properties.name: Nevada County Narrow Gauge (III)
properties.address: Railroad Ave at back entrance to Northern Queen Inn
properties.description: For 66 years, the Nevada City depot served as the northern terminus for the railroad affectionately known as the 'Never Come Never Go.' In 1874 a group of 7 men, led by lumber mill and gold mine owner John C. Coleman, organized the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad. Engineer John Flint Kidder was hired on January 14, 1875 to build a 23-mile narrow gauge railroad from the Central Pacific Railroad connection in Colfax to Grass Valley and Nevada City. On a snowy May 20, 1876, John Coleman drove the last spike in Nevada City to dedicate the completion of the railroad. John Kidder controlled the railroad from January 1884, until his death on April 10, 1901; the following day, his widow Sara was elected president of the railroad. Sara Kidder successfully managed the company until its sale on April 24, 1913 to the Oakland, Antioch and Eastern, a predecessor to the Western Pacific. On May 13, 1926, the N.C.N.G.R.R. was purchased by a group of local investors and it operated until December 1942. The scrapping of the railroad was completed by May 5,1943. Two other plaques at this location were dedicated on June 11,1994, but cannot presently be located. Text of second plaque: In fond appreciation of the known surviving employees of the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad Co. 1876-1942. Bob Paine, Tony Dellea, Sadie Angelini, Eugene Katzer, Homer Bevared, George Jefferson, Edward Smith, Bill Tremewan. Text of third plaque: In grateful appreciation to the Northern Queen Inn and the Ramey family for their dedicated efforts in the restoration and preservation of Nevada Countyâ€™s railroad history.

properties.name: Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad (I)
properties.address: Entrance at Bennett and Bank Sts. Plaque at Bennett and Kidder Sts.
properties.description: Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad ran from here 5 miles N to Nevada City and 17 miles S to Colfax. Built 1874-76 by John F. Kidder whose home is NE corner. Depot and shops were 50 yards N., rails removed 1942. Sarah, widow of builder, was first woman R.R. president.

properties.name: Nevada County Narrow Gauge RR (II)
properties.description: Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad was dedicated May 20, 1876 one hundred feet east of this spot. Built by John Flint Kidder between 1874-76 it ran 22 miles from Nevada City to Colfax via Grass Valley. Rails taken up in 1942. Sarah Kidder, widow of builder, worldÃ

properties.name: Nevada County Traction Company
properties.address: Corner of Pine and Broad Sts
properties.description: Nevada County Traction Company 1901-1924. Our 28-ton standard gauge street cars ran from this spot five miles to Boston Ravine south of Grass Valley. Car barns and power plant located at Glenbrook mid-way. Line was victim of motor cars and busses.

properties.name: Nevada Theatre
properties.description: Opened Sept. 11, 1865 with comic Dan Setchell and dancers Worrell Sisters. Built by Geo. S. Pierce. Mark Twain, Emma Nevada, Jack London, Gilbert Barry and Richard Jose performed here. Movies shown from 1908-1958 Acquired 1967 by Liberal Arts Commission for restoration. Second plaque dedicated 1974 by State of California in cooperation with the Nevada County Historical Landmarks and Liberal Arts Commissions: CaliforniaÃ

properties.name: North Bloomfield Mining & Gravel Co.
properties.address: Malakoff Diggins State Park; 0.8 m. west of North Bloomfield on No. Bloomfield Rd
properties.description: Landmark registered 1972 by State of California, CHL 852. Plaque dedicated 1972 by State in cooperation with Chap 10 E Clampus Vitus and Malakoff Citizens Advisory Committee: This was a major hydraulic operation in California. Its vast system of canals and flumes using a drainage tunnel 7800 feet long was termed a feat of engineering skill. It was the principal defendant in an anti-debris lawsuit settled by the Sawyer decision in 1884. SawyerÃ

properties.name: North Bloomfield School
properties.address: North side No. Bloomfield Rd, west end of North Bloomfield
properties.description: North Bloomfield School 1872-73. Built at a cost of some 3,000 dollars. Modeled after schoolhouses at nearby Sebastopol and Sweetland, it served the community until closed in 1941. Acquired from Washington School District, Nevada County, for public use and display. Restoration commenced in 1973 by popular subscription.

properties.name: North San Juan
properties.address: In North San Juan on front of Bigley's Market, Hwy 49
properties.description: North San Juan 1853. Dedicated to the memory of the founder Christian Keinz and to all the pioneer argonauts of San Juan Ridge, who by their labor and fortitude contributed so greatly to the wealth and economy of Nevada County, California and the nation.

properties.name: North San Juan Fire Department
properties.address: 10057 Reservoir St, North San Juan, off Hwy 49
properties.description: First Fire Department. To the memory of the first fire department, organized 1862, George Murphy, first fire chief. Note: The building is now a community hall.

properties.name: North San Juan Methodist Episcopal Church
properties.address: End of Flume St off Hwy 49
properties.description: Erected in 1857 at a cost of $1500, the structure Is the earliest existing church in the county. It contains early furnishings. Second plaque dedicated 1980 by the church board and Chap 10 E Clampus Vitus: North San Juan United Methodist Church 1856. One of the oldest of its faith in continuous operation in California. Escaped three major fires which destroyed the town. The Rev. H. B. Sheldon, first assigned pastor, rode circuit from Brandy City to French Corral on horseback.

properties.name: North Star House
properties.description: This 1905 structure was built for Arthur D. Foote, manager of the North Star mine from 1895 to 1913, and his wife, Mary Hallock Foote, a noted 19th-century author and illustrator. The structure was designed by Julia Morgan, Californiaâ€™s first licensed female architect and designer of the Hearst properties at San Simeon, Asilomar in Pacific Grove, and numerous buildings on the U. C. Berkeley campus, and in total, over 700 buildings. The property is representative of the era that brought Nevada County to the forefront of gold production in California.

properties.name: North Star Mine Powerhouse and Nevada County Traction Company
properties.address: Allison Ranch Rd at McCourtney Rd
properties.description: The North Star Powerhouse, built by A. D. Foote in 1895, was the first complete water powered compressed air transmission plant of its kind. Compressed air, generated by Pelton water wheels, furnished power for the entire mine operation. The 30-foot Pelton wheel was the largest in the world, and was in continuous use for over 30 years. Second plaque dedicated 1991 by American Society of Mechanical Engineers: International Mechanical Engineering Heritage Collection Pelton Water Wheel Collection North Star Power House Museum. The Pelton Wheel uses the momentum of a water jet impinging on buckets attached to the periphery of a wheel to produce power. It is a development of the primitive, so-called 'Hurdy Gurdy' wheel used in the California goldfields in the nineteenth century. Various inventors have contributed this type of water turbine, including Lester A. Pelton (1831-1908), who in 1880 patented the characteristic split bucket form. Subsequent important contributions to Pelton-Wheel technology were made by W. A. Doble. This collection illustrates the origin and development of the Pelton Waterwheel. American Society of Mechanical Engineers-1991.Third plaque dedicated 1997 by Historical Society near smaller wheel: Pelton Type Water Wheel. Produced 71000 hp at 327 rpm, diameter 10 ft. Weight 26,000 lbs. Built by Allis Chalmers of single cast nickel steel. Gift of Oroville-Wyandotte Irrigation Dist. Note: The building presently houses the Historical Society's Mining Museum.

properties.name: Old Auburn Road Bridge (Site)
properties.address: Old Auburn Rd, southeast of modern bridge over Wolf Creek about 4 m. south of McCourtney Rd
properties.description: The Old Auburn Bridge was registered as a landmark in 1996 by Nevada County. The bridge consisted of a single span, pin-connected steel pony truss, 66-feet in length, 11-feet in width, with a timber deck carrying a single traffic lane. It was built in 1897 by Cotton Brothers and Co. of Oakland, California. In 2004, the old bridge was dismantled by the County as unsafe and replaced by a modern span. Recognizing the historical significance of the spot, the County erected a monument containing a bronze plaque: Historic Marker Perrin Crossing. This bridge replaces the original steel truss span constructed in 1897 and remained in continuous service until 2004. This crossing received its name from Joseph and Susan Perrin, who settled nearby in 1851. Close to this crossing the Perrin family operated a stagecoach stop known for its hospitality. This road was part of the original stagecoach route between Sacramento and Grass Valley. Note: Remains of the stagecoach stop can be seen about 100 ft. south of the monument

properties.name: Old Bear River Bridge
properties.address: Hwy 174 at Bear River, between Colfax and Chicago Park
properties.description: Since 1852 bridges near this site have served as the gateway to western Nevada County. This 1924 span was the first concrete bridge at this site and features a Roman style single arch design, constructed in a continuous pour method. The Colfax Highway Association led a citizens group that saved the bridge from the wreckerâ€™s ball in 1986, and placed this monument in 1992.

properties.name: Old Brick Gothic Church Building
properties.address: Corner of Main and Church Sts
properties.description: Historic Old Brick Gothic Building. Constructed as a Congregational Church in 1857. Restored by First Baptist Church. Notes: Also site of Nevada Cityâ€™s pioneer nondenominational church, which burned in March 1851. It was replaced by a wooden Congregational Church, that burned in 1856. Cornerstone of this brick building was laid on July 4, 1858 (not 1857.) The roof and interior burned in 1863 and the church was rebuilt once more in 1864. The University of California was born at the church in 1853, when Henry Durant took part in a convention of Congregationalists and Presbyterians who resolved to establish a college in California. Durant soon opened the Contra Costa Academy in Oakland, that become the College of California then relocated to Berkeley as the University of California.

properties.name: Old Capitol Building
properties.description: The Old Capitol. Erected in 1870. In its heyday it was the social center of Truckee . . . Many of the leading thespians . . . politicians . . . and other celebrities of that era gathered here . . . Note: This plaque has disappeared. A plaque placed on the front of building by unknown persons reads: Capitol Building 1870 Truckee California. The first brick building in Truckee, it was originally Hurd's Saloon, then the Capitol Saloon, later used by the Piper Opera Company and the County Court.

properties.name: Old Chinese Herb Shop
properties.address: Corner of S.E. River St. and Brockway Rd.
properties.description: Old Chinatown. Construction of the Central Pacific Railroad over Donner Summit brought thousands of Chinese laborers here in 1868. They used â€˜squatter's rights' to build a ramshackle colony along the Truckee River on what is now River Avenue East. A huge fire destroyed Chinatown on May 29, 1875, but the Orientals rebuilt their community. Finally, on Nov. 18, 1878, a group of about 500 citizens, under the leadership of the Caucasian League, tore down the shanty town and ordered the 1000 or more Chinese to leave. They simply moved across the river, adjoining this former site of a Chinese herb shop, and built anew. A third fire, on Jan. 3, 1883, consumed the 'new' Chinatown and brought about the gradual end to the Chinese colony here. Bronze plaque dedicated 1980 by Chap 3691 E Clampus Vitus. Text of bronze plaque: Old Chinese Herb Shop.

properties.name: Old North San Juan School
properties.address: 29190 Hwy 49, south end of North San Juan
properties.description: The first school building on this site was moved from Flume Street in 1869. It burned in 1924, and this building was moved here from Lake Vera. This site operated as a school from 1869 until 1984. It is currently used as a community center and library.

properties.name: Old Pacific Fruit Packing Shed
properties.address: Mount Olive and Lower Colfax Rds, Chicago Park
properties.description: A smaller shed, built here in 1926, collapsed during a snowstorm in 1929. Its successor was built in 1930 by the Civilian Conservation Corps. The Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad tracks, depot, and water tower were alongside this building, where peaches and pears from the surrounding orchards were packed and sent to Colfax by train. There the boxes were transferred to standard sized refrigerator freight cars to be shipped to the East.

properties.name: Old Stone Garage
properties.address: Southwest corner Jibboom and Bridge Sts
properties.description: Truckeeâ€™s Old Stone Garage and Site of Townâ€™s First Dwelling. In 1863, Joseph Gray built Truckee's first structure on this site for use as the Dutch Flat-Donner Lake Wagon Road's toll station. The old log structure was moved in 1907 to the Englehart property on Church Street, one block east of this site, where it still stands and the Brethren of ECV dedicated a plaque in 1965. In 1909, D. J. Smith, with the help of European-trained stone masons, constructed the present building for use as a livery and garage.

properties.name: Original Ott Assay Office Site
properties.description: Site of the Original Ott Assay Office. J. J. Ott's assay in 1859 helped develop the great Comstock Lode and the State of Nevada. Note: Ottâ€™s original Assay Office was built of wood. It burned in the fire of 1863 and Ott moved into the existing brick building next door.

properties.name: Ormonde Town Site
properties.address: Maybert Rd, 3.6 m. east of Washington. About 3/10 m. past Maybert (Canyon Creek) bridge in Golden Quartz campground. Maybert Rd does not require 4-wheel-drive but not advised for cars with low clearance.
properties.description: The town of Ormonde was established here in 1887. Downstream, across the mouth of Canyon Creek was Canyonville 1850, and upriver approximately four miles was Maybert 1886. These towns were part of the upper Washington Mining District and were the furthest upstream of the serious mining ventures along the South Yuba. During its brief history the district yielded 17 million dollars in gold, but by 1897 it was nearly totally abandoned. The remains have since been obliterated by flooding. Note: The town consisted of 8-10 buildings, including a store, saloon and boarding house.

properties.name: Overland Emigrant Trail--Highway 49
properties.address: Hwy 49 south of Grass Valley, 1.2 m. north of Combie Rd between Clivus Dr and Brewer Rd at South Wolf Creek bridge
properties.description: At this point, the old Overland Emigrant Trail approaches the present highway. More than 100-years ago it resounded to the creaking wheels of pioneer wagons and the cry of hardy but footsore travelers buoyed by the realization that their long trip to the goldfields was about over.

properties.name: Pelton Water Wheel
properties.description: No. 4, 1928-1987. Produced 18,000 HP at 257 RPM and enough electrical power for 16,000 households. 12 ft. diameter, weight 15 tons. Built in San Francisco by Pelton Company. A Constitutional Bicentennial gift to Nevada City by Pacific Gas and Electric.

properties.name: Pine Street Crossing at Nevada City
properties.address: S. Pine St at Deer Creek
properties.description: Pine Street Bridge 1996. Third span of Deer Creek at this location. It is a replica of the single arch steel Gault bridge (1903-1996) which replaced the suspension bridge (1862-1903) built by Andrew S. Hallidie, who later built the San Francisco cable car system. Note: The bridge is officially named the South Pine Street Bridge at Hallidie Crossing. Hallidie's patented structure collapsed two weeks after it opened, killing two men. The Gault bridge, similar to the one at Edwards Crossing (No. 128) was named for Nevada City Mayor Alexander Gault.

properties.name: Pioneer Cemetery
properties.address: East side of W. Broad St, near Hwy 49
properties.description: This cemetery was established in 1851 on a knoll back of the First Methodist Church. This gave the site its name. Many noted citizens rest here. Note: The first cemetery in Nevada County after arrival of the 49ers. Located in the churchyard of the first Methodist Church that once occupied this site, it has been in use since 1851. A wooden marker was placed in 1949. The plaque is on a stone monument in the front part of the cemetery. Many famous pioneers and early settlers are buried here, including Henry Meredith and William Alphonso Sutter, the son of General John Sutter. Aaron A. Sargent's cenotaph is here though his remains were cremated and scattered by air above the Quaker Hill Mine, which he used to own.

properties.name: Pioneer Emigrant Trail (I)
properties.address: On Airport Rd about 100 feet from junction with Lake Vera Rd
properties.description: A branch of the Pioneer Emigrant Trail leading from Truckee Pass to the gold mines of Nevada County and to the Valley of the Sacramento divided on Harmony Ridge near the site of Lone Grave. One route circled the north horizon to Blue Tent, down to Selby Flat, up through this gap and on south.

properties.name: Pioneer Emigrant Trail (II)
properties.address: 10867 Coyote St, near North Bloomfield Rd
properties.description: A branch of the Pioneer Emigrant Trail leading from Truckee Pass to the gold mines of Nevada County traversed Harmony Ridge down through this gap and on to the Valley of the Sacramento.

properties.name: Pioneers Memorial
properties.address: Memorial Park, corner of Race St and Colfax Ave
properties.description: Plaque dedicated 1929 by Native Daughters of the Golden West: In memory of our pioneer mothers & fathers dedicated June 12, 1929 by Manzanita Parlor No 29 N.D.G.W.

properties.name: Plaza Grocery
properties.description: This store, opened in 1882 by Tom Shurtleff, contained the only water-powered elevator in Nevada County. Longest continuous operation of any Nevada City grocery until it closed in 1973. Note: Building was a boarding house from 1880 to 1882. Celio family bought it in 1910 and leased to Scheemers in 1928. John Sbaffi bought the business in 1949 and purchased the building in 1965. It was a grocery until 1973, when 'The Jacks' leased it for use as a restaurant. It has been used primarily as a bar or restaurant since.

properties.name: Pollard's Station
properties.address: Near intersection of S. Shore Dr and Donner Pass Rd, west of Truckee
properties.description: On this site, Joseph Delos Pollard constructed the first resort on the west end of Donner Lake. Between mid 1864 and early 1868 Pollardâ€™s Station on the Dutch Flat & Donner Lake Wagon Road was office to the Pioneer Stage Co. and Wells, Fargo & Co. Express, as well as a hotel, boasting bar room, reading room, billiards and a bowling alley. Visitors could rent boats, horses or carriages to enjoy the scenic grandeur. The station was ravaged by fire twice and rebuilt once. The station was no longer needed due to the completion of the railroad in June 1868, which put a virtual end to wagon and stage traffic across the Sierra.

properties.name: Powell House
properties.address: Southeast corner Pine and Spring Sts
properties.description: Built in 1855 as the edifice of the Baptist Church. It was here that famed soprano Emma Nevada (Emma Wixom Palmer) made her debut at the age of three. In 1886 the church was remodeled as the residence of the E. T. R. Powell family. Note: The building now houses apartments.

properties.name: Purdon Crossing Bridge
properties.address: Bottom of Purdon Rd at South Fork of Yuba River, north of Nevada City
properties.description: Purdon Bridge 1895. Built by Cotton Brothers of Oakland, this steel bridge replaced several wooden bridges washed away in floods. It is the only bridge of the half-through truss design remaining in California. Purdon Crossing was a vital link of the main road from Nevada City to Downieville and the Northern Mines. Note: The unique quality of a half-through truss is that the deck is carried halfway between the top and bottom of the support structure; very few were built in California. First bridge at this site was built by William E. Robinson in 1854. In 1859 John Webber dismantled Robinson's Bridge and replaced it. Webber's Bridge washed away in December 1861 and was replaced by a rope suspension foot bridge in February 1862. Jesse Wall bought the foot bridge and crossing in the spring of 1862 and built a new bridge that summer. Wall sold it to James M. Black in 1864. Peter Purdon bought the bridge in 1870 and sold it to the county in 1890, which replaced it with this one in 1895.

properties.name: Railroad and Transportation Museum
properties.address: Five Kidder Court, Nevada City. Take Ridge Rd exit off Hwy 49
properties.description: The Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad (1876_1942) ran 22 miles from Nevada City through Grass Valley, its headquarters; to Colfax, Placer County, where it met the transcontinental line of the Central (later Southern) Pacific Railroad. Built by John F. Kidder, it was the main freight and passenger route serving the western county. Kidder ran the railroad until his death in 1901 and was followed by his widow Sarah, who became the first woman railroad president in United States. She served until 1913. This museum is dedicated to preserving the NCNGRR's place in history and to those who built, operated and maintained the little road. Second plaque dedicated 2011 by Native Sons of the Golden West: Railroad and Transportation Museum. The Nevada County Historical Society founded the Transportation Museum Division in 1983 to acquire, document, restore and preserve information and artifacts pertaining to Nevada County's railroad and transportation history. Best known is the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad that operated from 1876 until 1942. The museum opened for public enjoyment on March 17, 2003.

properties.name: Red Dog Town Site
properties.address: Red Dog Rd, 3 m. north of You Bet Rd intersection. 0.5 m. southeast of Greenhorn Creek
properties.description: Note: The site is a federally restricted area. Red Dog (1851-1871), with a population of 2,000 at its peak, was the third town of importance in Nevada County, surviving longer than most of the earliest gold towns. The Post Office closed in 1869.

properties.name: Red Ledge Stamp Mill
properties.address: Corner of Main and Auburn Sts
properties.description: Stamp Mill. This three stamp mill was donated to the City of Grass Valley by Mrs. Stella Tracy, owner of the famous Red Ledge Mine near Washington, Nevada County, where this mill crushed tons of gold bearing ore for many years. It is erected here in memory of the 'Cousin Jack' miners who came from Cornwall, England with their knowledge of hardrock mining used to bring gold bearing quartz to the surface from the deep mines of the area.

properties.name: Reliance Hose Company No. 3
properties.address: Race St near S. Auburn St
properties.description: This Building, Housing Reliance Hose Company No. 3 since 1891, is hereby marked for preservation, thus honoring The Volunteer Fire Department of Grass Valley March 26, 1988 100th Anniversary of Manzanita Parlor #29, Native Daughters of the Golden West. Note: Marion Leech Conway's memoirs indicate the building was dedicated in December 1911, after housing the company for twenty years. "The doors of Reliance No. 3 have had the latch string out in welcome greeting to all when the word went out the Reliance would entertain its friends."

properties.name: Relief Hill
properties.address: South of intersection of Relief Hill Rd and Relief Hill Mine Rd, about 5 m. east of North Bloomfield
properties.description: Note: Access to the old townsite and historic cemetery is sometimes restricted by the US Forest Service. Description: Relief Hill dates from 1853. By 1856, it had a population of 75, two saloons, one store, one butcher shop, one blacksmith shop, two boarding houses and several homes. In 1858, registered voters alone numbered 100. Its decline followed the Sawyer decision enjoining certain aspects of hydraulic mining. In the early 1900s, some residents moved closer to Relief Hill Road. Some of those buildings can still be seen. While there is still some intermittent mining in the area, by the 1960s, the old townsite was largely deserted. Not much but the cemetery remains.

properties.name: Robinson Plaza
properties.address: Foot of Main St, Nevada City. The plaza is dedicated to Beryl Robinson and contains several sites and plaques.
properties.description: Robinson Plaza named for Beryl Robinson Jr. Born in Nevada City September 13, 1935. City Councilman-City Manager. On July 1,1999, Beryl Robinson Jr. became the longest-serving City Manager in state history. A native of the town he has so ably served, Beryl was appointed City Manager on June 21, 1965. In addition to being Nevada City's chief administrative and financial official, he has diligently worked to preserve the community's 19th-century spirit and buildings, including the two historic structures here at Robinson Plaza. Beryl's continuing career in public service has already spanned the terms of seven presidents, six governors and three-dozen council members. A Mason, Rotarian and Elk, Beryl has brought a sense of commitment and purpose two his life matched by few and bettered by none.

properties.name: Rocking Stone
properties.address: Between High St and Keiser Ave, near Spring St
properties.description: A natural glacial formation, or the work of an unknown tribe as a form of altar. Its exact origin will never be known. The perfectly balanced stone until recently would rock at the touch of a finger. C. F. McGlashan built the original tower in 1895 and used it to display Donner Party relics and his famous butterfly collection. Site of an overnight stop for the Olympic Torch in 1960.

properties.name: Rollins Dam and Reservoir
properties.address: Larsen Rd about 1000 ft from Orchard Springs access road to Rollins Lake, Chicago Park
properties.description: Two plaques dedicated 1966 by Nevada Irrigation District: "Nevada Irrigation District, Yuba-Bear River Development, Rollins Dam & Reservoir Completed 1965.â€

properties.name: Root Cellar on Mexican War Land Grant
properties.address: 16330 Gibboney Lane, Grass Valley. Take Hwy 49 to Alta Sierra intersection and follow Alta Sierra Dr to Gibboney Lane
properties.description: This old root cellar is located on property that originally was part of a Land Grant of 160 acres granted to Josiah Dodge by President Ulysses Grant in 1873 as Bounty Land for his service as a private with Captain Steen's Co., 1st U.S. Dragoons, War with Mexico. In 1878 this property became part of the John Bree Ranch. The root cellar, built by Bree in the 1880s, was used to store his produce. Mr. Bree was among the first in the county to plant a Bartlett Pear orchard for commercial purposes.

properties.name: Rough and Ready Schoolhouse Site
properties.address: 10079 Blue Creek Rd, south side of Old Rough and Ready Hwy, opposite Cemetery entrance
properties.description: Note: Present owner requests privacy. Landmark: The original one-room Rough and Ready school was built in 1868. It served the community for many generations before being destroyed in the Trauner Fire of 1994. The house Kevin and Gaylene Collins built here in 1995 has an exterior like that of the schoolhouse.

properties.name: Rough and Ready Toll House
properties.description: This house and the adjacent property contain five plaques. First plaque: Built in 1859 by Benoni Thompson to collect tolls for using the Gephardt Union Turnpike running 6 miles from Penn Valley to Grass Valley. The last remaining original toll house in the West. It served as a Wells Fargo Express office and stage stop, and was the secret rendezvous place of Joaquin Murietta and Lola Montez. [Note: The reference to Joaquin Murietta and Lola Montez must be taken with a grain of salt and credited to the droll humor of E Clampus Vitus. There is no evidence of such a friendship, although anything was possible in the Old West.]

properties.name: Rough and Ready Town Site
properties.address: Rough and Ready Hwy, corner Chapel Rd, Rough and Ready
properties.description: Rough and Ready. Established In 1849, and named after the Rough and Ready Company of miners from Wisconsin, in honor of General Zachary Taylor, this was one of the principal towns of Nevada County. Here in 1850, articles of secession were drawn up establishing the 'Republic of Rough and Ready.' As a result of disastrous fires, only a few structures remain today of those built in the 1850s.

properties.name: Rowe Saw Mill
properties.address: 14365 Old Rough and Ready Hwy, Rough and Ready
properties.description: Fourth plaque: Rowe Saw Mill. In the 1860s there were four saw mills in this area. This display, in honor of his founding fathers, is from the Tom Rowe Saw Mill, and displays how the mill works. The foreman stood in the cage and called out the beam size (8x8, 12x12, etc.) then the cutter set the blade which was on a track, and rode the blade down the track until the beam cut was complete. The beam then went to the mines to build the shafts.

properties.name: Saint Canice Catholic Church
properties.description: Historic St. Canice Catholic Church was built and first services held in 1864 under the guidance of Father John Griffin. Note: This also is site of St. Peter and St. Paul Catholic Church, built in 1857 and destroyed by fire in 1863.

properties.name: Saint Columncille's Catholic Church
properties.address: No. Bloomfield Rd, west of North Bloomfield, Malakoff Diggins State Park
properties.description: The original plaque disappeared and was replaced in 1949 by Native Daughters of the Golden West by a wooden plaque: St. Columncilleâ€™s Catholic Church. Formerly the Bridgeport Union Guard Hall, was used in 1860 to train men for the Civil War under Capt. Frank Coffey of French Corral. In 1880, it was purchased as a house of worship for the Catholics of the ridge towns. Note: This building formerly was located at Birchville. In 1969 it was purchased by Babe Pinaglia and given to Malakoff Diggins Park, where it occupies the site of the Catholic Church that once served the miners of North Bloomfield.

properties.name: Saint Paul Lutheran Church Site
properties.address: St. Paul Pl. east of Hwy 174 at Chicago Park. St. Paul Cemetery is first left past the fire station
properties.description: On this site donated by Anna Pusheck the German Evangelical Lutheran St. Paul congregation was established in 1902 for worship and praise to God, continuing until 1984. Note: Anna Pusheck was the mother of Dr. Charles Pusheck, one of the Chicago Park Colony's developers. In 1895, he built a sanitorium in Chicago Park, which he later converted to a hotel. The St. Paul Lutheran church building was dedicated in December 1904. After the church was destroyed by fire on MotherÃ

properties.name: Schallenberger Cabin Site
properties.address: In Donner Memorial State Park, about 75 yds. east of Museum
properties.description: Near this spot stood a small cabin built by 18-year old Moses Schallenberger and two other men. They were members of the Stephens-Townsend-Murphy party of 1844, the first pioneers to take wagons over the Sierra Nevada, opening the Truckee Route of the California Trail. The three men had volunteered to remain behind and guard six of the wagons left here by the main party. Due to extreme winter weather conditions and lack of food, the three men agreed to separate. The two older men rejoined the wagon party, but due to his weakened physical condition, Moses Schallenberger chose to remain at the cabin. The young man survived for nearly three months alone in the small cabin during the winter of 1844-45 before being rescued. His cabin would later provide shelter for the Breen family, members of the ill-fated Donner Party, who were stranded here in 1846-47. The courage and resolution of Moses Schallenberger during his solitary winter ordeal in this cabin makes him one of the true heroes in the saga of the California Trail. This monument was made possible through the generosity of many groups and individuals committed to the preservation of the California Trail. Note: The monument contains a time capsule to be opened on August 19, 2095.

properties.name: Searls Law Office
properties.description: This building completed December 18, 1872, was the law office for three generations of this family. Niles Searls, who arrived in 1851, practiced law here from 1872 to 1885. He had been elected district attorney in 1855, then district judge for Nevada, Sierra and Yuba counties, then became Chief Justice of the California Supreme Court in 1887. Fred Searls, his son, held the longest tenure in this building (1879-1929). He drafted the first incorporation papers of the hydroelectric industry of California. Carroll Searls, son of Fred, practiced here from 1915 to 1923; later became general counsel of the Newmont Mining Company in New York City; used this office until November 4,1970. Note: Niles Searls was elected district attorney in 1852, not 1855, but because he left California and went east to marry before his term of office began, William M. Stewart was appointed to fill the vacancy. Later, when Stewart resigned in 1854 to become the acting state attorney general, Searls was appointed to replace him as district attorney. From 1972 until 2014 the building housed the Historical SocietyÃ

properties.name: Shaar Zedek Grass Valley Jewish Cemetery
properties.address: Location: Second Street off Eureka Street
properties.description: Shaar Zedek, or the Gate of Righteousness, was established in 1856. The first burial was in 1857 and the last in 1891. Thirty headstones are visible. The cemetery was restored around 50 years ago by the Commission for the Preservation of Pioneer Jewish Cemeteries and Landmarks. It is locked and fenced to protect against vandalism.

properties.name: Sheriff David Fulton Douglass
properties.address: Just past 15934 Airport Rd, 3/4 m. from junction with Lake Vera Rd, Nevada City
properties.description: On this spot Sheriff Douglass, a Native Son of the Golden West, gave his life July 26, 1896, bravely performing his duty. Alone he tracked a highwayman to this retreat and both fell In battle. It is believed Douglass was pitted against two and that one escaped. The bodies lay parallel. Nearby is a second plaque identifying the spot where the bandit lay.

properties.name: Shoot (Chute) Hill
properties.address: Across from space 29 in Chute Hill campground about 7/10 m. east of North Bloomfield
properties.description: Local historians relate that water from high mountain lakes flowed thru a 500' wooden 'chute' which led to a huge square tank or pressure box behind campsite 29. Here it entered a 3' pipe reduced to gain pressure to 2', then down hill 200' to the hydraulic monitors at the mine.

properties.name: Sierra Mountain Cemetery
properties.address: East end of E. Jibboom St
properties.description: The Masonic and Odd Fellows lodges each established cemeteries here in 1869 or 1870 to provide a burial place for their deceased. Catholics established a cemetery to the southwest. Pioneers and prominent citizens were buried within the original cemetery grounds. The less reputable were buried outside the fences. Stories abound of the bodies placed in snowbanks or ice houses awaiting the ground thaw. The Truckee Cemetery District was formed in 1946 and received title to the land and named the cemetery in 1964. In 1954 the original site increased in size despite the best efforts of the community to prevent the townfolk from dying.

properties.name: Sierra Sun (Truckee Republican)
properties.description: Oldest continuous business enterprise in the Central Sierra commenced publication in 1869 as the 'Truckee Republican' and was also informally known as the 'Truckee Screwdriver.' The definitive 'History of the Donner Party' was first published in this newspaper in 1879 when author, teacher, statesman C. F. McGlashan was its editor. Note: The Truckee Republican, which began life as the Grass Valley Republican, commenced publishing at Truckee in 1872, not 1869. Its descendant is now the Sierra Sun. The plaque was moved in 2014 from 12315 Deerfield Drive, and cannot currently be located.

properties.name: South Yuba Canal Office
properties.description: Headquarters for the largest network of water flumes and ditches In the state. The South Yuba Canal Water Company was the first incorporated to supply water for hydraulic mining. The original ditch was in use in May, 1850, and this company office was in use from 1857 to 1880. The holdings later became the vast P.G. & E. hydro-electric system.Second plaque dedicated 1987 by Native Sons of the Golden West: US Constitution 1787 Bicentennial 1987 Nevada City, a Bicentennial Community. The plaques are mounted on adjacent brick buildings. The one on the left originally was the Potter Building and the other began life as the City Drugstore, Both survived the great fire of 1856 and are two of the oldest in Nevada City. The South Yuba Canal Water Company did not occupy the building until 1861.

properties.name: St Patrick's Cemetery
properties.description: St. Patrickâ€™s Cemetery 1853. In memory of the pioneers buried here. A sign nearby contains this information: Site of the first Catholic Church in Grass Valley. Dedicated on October 25,1853. Rev. John Shanahan, 1st Pastor. Served as church 'till December 5, 1858. Served as the first Catholic School (1859-1879).

properties.name: Stampede Circle of Stones
properties.address: 10600 Brockway Rd, northeast corner of Truckee River Regional Park, Truckee
properties.description: The Stampede Circle of Stones is the oldest manmade structure in the Truckee region-at least 500 years old. Built by the Washoe or their predecessors, the Martis people, the Circle's original home was in Stampede Valley, about 10 miles northeast of here. In 1969 it was moved to this location shortly before the construction of the Stampede Dam flooded the valley. Archaeological digging on the original site indicates it may have been used as an antelope corral.

properties.name: Summit City/Meadow Lake
properties.address: Meadow Lake Rd, south of Jackson Meadows Rd
properties.description: In June of 1863, the trapper Henry Hartley discovered gold bearing ore here and staked out the Excelsior Company claims. Exaggerated reports and a 1865 market panic in the Comstock mines of Virginia City brought thousands of miners overnight to the supposed 'Little Comstock' of Summit City. Within two years an incorporated town, renamed Meadow Lake grew to encompass 160 acres with 4,000 summer residents and over 500 buildings, including 200 business houses, a stock exchange, daily newspaper, Post Office, school, 6 breweries and 80 saloons. Streets were laid out grid fashion, with a 9,000 square foot plaza on the lakeshore. An excursion vessel ferried revelers to four hurdy-gurdy houses at the lakeâ€™s upper end. The winter of 1866-1867 brought 30 feet of snow with the 'Snowshoe Express' delivering mail through Cisco. In all the eight stamp mills in a district of 10,910 claims failed to extract the expected gold from its 'Rebellious Ore.' The optimism faded and the town declined with like rapidity. July of 1869 found 60 residents. By 1873 only Hartley 'The Hermit' remained. His grave is on a hillside to the west of town. A wooden sign nearby was erected at an unknown date by Troop 40, Kentfield-Marin Council, Boy Scouts of America: Meadow Lake-Excelsior. The granite dam was built 1858 for flumes of valley gold towns. Called Summit City with 2000 pop. after 1866. It incorporated as Meadow Lake 1866. Because gold could not be separated it was abandoned 1872.

properties.name: Ten Stamp Mill
properties.address: City parking lot on Commercial St, west of York St
properties.description: 'Six tall, upright rods of iron, as large as a manÃ

properties.name: The Auditorium Building
properties.description: The Auditorium Building. On July 30, 1896, a fire started here at the site of Argallâ€™s Opera House, owned by Charles E. Clinch, burning the entire block on East Mill Street from Neal to Bank. In 1900, an incorporation composed of the Native Sons of the Golden West, Ancient Order of the Foresters, Knights of Pythias, Red Men, and the Minerâ€™s Union was formed to build a fine theater of three stories. The first floor held a magnificent theater with a capacity of 600 on the floor and 250 in the gallery and office space for the Grass Valley City Hall. The second story was devoted to lodge rooms and the third story was a banquet hall. The basement housed both the kitchen and the armory for Company 1 of the National Guard. On February 2, 1901, the Auditorium Theater opened to a week-long celebration and operated until November 12, 1922. Two days later, it reopened as the Strand Movie Theater, operating until April 22, 1942. The building was then home of the F. S. Rasco & Co. store and since 1972 has been home to Hedman/Ashley furniture store.

properties.name: The Flagg Building
properties.description: The Flagg Bldg. The Eternal Saloon. Built in 1856 on the site of the U.S. Hotel, this brick edifice has served Nevada City as the council chamber, Schreiber's, and since 1957 Eddie Furano's Bank Club. Here at Schreiberâ€™s Felix, the marvelous, mysterious music box thundered Strauss waltzes and 'Carmen' to generations with custom and good cheer.

properties.name: The Old Post Office
properties.description: This building Is known as the Old Post Office. It was built in 1914 by the United States Government as the Grass Valley Post Office. From 1914 until 1984 the Post Office functioned for mail, package and gold shipments from the many mines in the area. After the Post Office moved to East Main Street the building operated as an Insurance Agency in the 1980â€™s and as professional offices since October 1989. Note: Plaque was dedicated in memory of Nevada County pioneer United States Senators Aaron Augustus Sargent (1873-1879) and William Morris Stewart (1865-1875 and 1887-1905).

properties.name: The Red Castle
properties.description: Nevada Cityâ€™s earliest surviving brick home, built in 1860 by Judge John Williams, mine owner, businessman and civic leader, who with his family, crossed the plains in 1849. Near ruin, this house was restored by James Wade Schaar in 1963. The Red Castle stands today as a proud reminder of the part the Argonauts played in the Heritage of California.

properties.name: The Tin Can -- Dot's
properties.description: The two modern buildings replaced structures built in the early 20th-century and used in two of Truckeeâ€™s notorious industries: bootlegging and prostitution. They upheld a tradition begun on this street in the 1870â€™s. Many a thirst was slaked and need fulfilled because of these buildings' contents. Politely ignored, although they never failed because of lack of business, they operated until mid-century to serve the working men of this and surrounding communities. Changing commercial interests and ultimately property values doomed these buildings to history.

properties.name: The Union Newspaper
properties.address: 11464 Sutton Way, Grass Valley, in the Glenbrook Basin
properties.description: Of the many historic newspapers started in the Northern Mines of the great Mother Lode during the famous gold rush era, only The Union of Grass Valley and Nevada City emerged a successful daily published for 100 years. Born in critical times: Civil War and presidential election. The Union has ever strived for unity and has evolved an independent voice supporting man and measure over partisanship. The Union is only the seventh daily newspaper in California to celebrate its Centennial under the original name. Centennial Date, October 28, 1964. Plaque dedicated when The Union newspaper was located on Mill Street, Grass Valley and moved with the newspaper to Sutton Way in December 1978.

properties.name: Theodore Dehone Judah
properties.address: Track side of Amtrak station, 10065 Donner Pass Rd
properties.description: As Chief Engineer of the Central Pacific Railroad, Judah's engineering skill, political savvy and financial genius, along with the promoters' capital, culminated in the formation and construction of the Trans-Sierra leg of the first transcontinental railroad. While fighting the excesses of the railroadÃ

properties.name: Toll Road Stake
properties.address: 14365 Old Rough and Ready Hwy, Rough and Ready
properties.description: Second Plaque: Toll Road Stake. Across the street from this location is a tree stump in which the original stake exists that was used to close off the passage to travelers from Grass Valley to Penn Valley. Drivers who frequented this road had to stop at this location and pay the toll. Once appropriate compensation was provided, the gate would be opened to allow passage.

properties.name: Town of Washington
properties.address: Bottom of Washington Rd on South Fork of the Yuba River, 18 m. northeast of Nevada City via Hwy 20
properties.description: Washington, originally Indiana Boys Camp, is a town that refused to ghost. It was settled in 1849. By 1880 miners had recovered over $10,500,000 in gold here. Note: First plaque is on the Washington Hotel. Note: The only mining town in the county not destroyed by fire. Today, Washington (pop. about 180) is one of the few remaining early mining towns. The permanent population swells to hundreds more residents in the summer months. A second plaque, shown above, was dedicated at an unknown date in a small park at the intersection of Washington Rd and Alpha Toll Rd: In August, 1850 Washington was the highest point on the South Yuba River at which gold had been discovered, with over a 1,000 miners. In 1870 the Chinese outnumbered the white population. The last try to strike it rich was in the 1890â€™s. Washington is the last remaining village of the many that flourished in the Washington Mining District. Historical notes by Robert I. and Grace I. Slyter.

properties.name: Trans-Sierra Freeway Project
properties.address: Highway rest stops on both sides of I-80, at Donner Summit, west of Truckee
properties.description: Plaque dedicated 1966 by the American Society of Civil Engineering: Civil Engineering Achievement of Merit, 1966, Awarded by the American Society of Civil Engineers.

properties.name: Trinity Episcopal Church
properties.address: Corner of Nevada and High Sts
properties.description: Trinity Episcopal Church. Founded as a parish in 1855, opened for Divine Service in this building on November 2, 1873. Previously on this site was â€˜Caldwell's Upper Store' built in 1849 for the miners of Upper Deer Creek Dry Diggings, now known as Nevada City, and Washington School, 1863, the communityâ€™s first high school. The present building was erected by shipwrights working in the mines. Their craftsmanship may be seen in its construction. Notes: CaldwellÃ

properties.name: Truckee Flag Pole
properties.address: 10065 Donner Pass Rd, northwest side of Amtrak station
properties.description: Truckee / Jewel of the Eastern Sierras. First known as Gray's Toll Station, then Coburn's Station, Truckee was established in 1863. Renowned for its lumber industry, transcontinental railroad, icehouses, saloons and red light district, Truckee has never been known for her laid back demeanor. It has always been a town where people came to entertain themselves on Friday and Saturday nights, just as it is today. Second plaque dedicated 1994 by Truckee Fire Protection District: Truckee Fire Protection District. This flagpole represents 100 years of service by the Truckee Fire Protection District and its members, past and present for their commitment to the preservation of life and property.

properties.name: Truckee Jail
properties.address: Northeast corner of Spring and Jibboom Sts
properties.description: 1875 Old Truckee Jail. Built in September 1875 and in continuous use until 1964. Landmark registered and second plaque dedicated 1971 by Nevada County, NEV 04: The first extension of the Nevada County jail which was built in 1875 by order of the County Supervisors on the petition of residents in Meadow Lake and Truckee townships due to the high cost of transporting prisoners from the eastern part of the county to the jail in Nevada City. Built of stone at a cost of $2,500 the building housed its first prisoner in September 1875. Note: Building now houses the Truckee-Donner Historical Museum.

properties.name: Truckee Round House Site
properties.address: East end of municipal parking lot, just west of 10021 Donner Pass Rd
properties.description: Needed to house and maintain the engines of the Central Pacific Railroad during construction of the trans-Sierra crossing, an enclosed wooden round house was constructed here in 1868. It was razed by fire in March of 1869. A 16-stall wooden structure was built that year as the maintenance facility of the C.P.R.R., Truckee Division. Another fire in 1876 resulted in construction of a 22-stall granite and wooden structure. This served through 1942, when it was officially retired, a victim of the end of the steam era and consolidation of maintenance at other locations. The round houses were home to helper engines, fire trains and the first rotary snow plow in the Sierra.

properties.name: Truckee's First Log Cabin
properties.description: First Log Cabin. Erected by Joseph Gray at S.W. corner of present day Bridge and Spring streets to serve Dutch Flat Wagon Road traffic over Donner Pass. Removed to this site 1907.

properties.name: Truckee's Vigilance ("601") Committee
properties.address: On sidewalk in front of 10096 Donner Pass Rd, across street from Amtrak Station,
properties.description: Truckeeâ€™s vigilance committee, known as the 601, was born in response to the rise in crime and 'undesirables' which plagued the young railroad town. In late 1874 and early 1875, the 601 carried out a campaign to intimidate and rid the town of those elements. After accidentally shooting one of their own members, the 601 disbanded until August 1889 when they reassembled for one last time. Though their goals were never fully achieved, the 601 did bring a semblance of law and order to the area through their vigilance.

properties.name: Veterans Memorial Flagstaffs, Grass Valley
properties.address: Entrances to Greenwood and Catholic cemeteries, W. Main St, Grass Valley
properties.description: Plaques and flagstaffs dedicated 1954 by Post 130, American Legion Auxiliary: Lest We Forget. This memorial flagstaff dedicated in eternal memory of those war veterans resting within the portals of this cemetery who served in the wars of our nation and those who gave their lives defending AmericaÃ

properties.name: Victory Highway Monument
properties.address: Northwest corner of Spring St and Donner Pass Rd, Truckee
properties.description: First plaque dedicated 1928. Text of plaque: Dedicated to Californiaâ€™s sons and daughters who served their country in the World War 1917-1918 and to the memory of those who gave the last full measure of devotion. Second plaque and monument dedicated 1998 by Truckee Town Council and California Department of Transportation: Victory Highway Monument Rededication July 24, 1998. This monument was originally sited on old Highway 40 near the California/Nevada line in 1928 and removed in the mid 1970s due to vandalism. It was one of a series of monuments placed on the 'Victory Highway' across the U.S., honoring those who died serving the United States in World War I. Donner Pass Road and part of Glenshire Drive through Truckee still follow the basic alignment of the old highway. The Town of Truckee, in cooperation with CalTrans, has rededicated this monument not only for its original purpose and the history of Old Highway 40, but also for current revitalization efforts occurring in historic downtown Truckee.

properties.name: Vietnam War Memorials, Grass Valley
properties.address: Memorial Park, corner of Race St and Colfax Ave
properties.description: Plaques dedicated 1966 and 1974 by Hague-Thomas-Hegarty (now Frank Gallino) Post No. 130, American Legion: 1966 plaque: Gary Ames Miller Memorial. This memorial is dedicated to all American servicemen and women of the Vietnam War in the name of Gary Ames Miller, United States Marine Corps, the first local serviceman to give the full measure for God and country in this conflict. All Americans owe an endless debt of gratitude to the gallant men and women of our armed services for preserving our way of life, a life endowed with justice, freedom and democracy. 1974 plaque: Western Nevada County servicemen killed in action in Vietnam: Edward Alien Ownry 09/09/65, Gary Ames Miller 06/01/66, Douglas A. Rix 02/28/67, Bruce Allan Jensen 08/27/67, Alan Robert Haugen 04/06/68, Ronald J. Walber 04/25/68, Thomas W. Cranford 05/09/68, Harry Lee Theurkauf 06/12/68, Donald Ray Webb 10/12/68, Ernest James Stidham 12/22/68, John R. Kunkel 01/13/69, Philip A. Tritsch 01/28/69, Kenneth W. Scurr 05/31/69, Michael D. Goeller 06/01/69, David E. Freestone 08/27/69, James Deeble 04/17/70. Note: These plaques are mounted on a huge drill core. There are also memorial plaques for the following Vietnam veterans located on the following Golden Center Freeway overpasses around Grass Valley. L/Cpl USMC: Gary Ames Miller 12/14/1947-06/01/1966, west end Brunswick Rd Pvt USA: Thomas W. Cranford 12/09/194-05/09/1968, west end Dorsey Dr S/Sgt USA: Douglas A. Rix 10/02/1942-02/28/196, north side Idaho_Maryland Rd L/Cpl USMC: John R. Kunkel 04/16/1947-01/13/1969, west side Bennett St 1st Lt USA: Kenneth W. Scurr 07/12/1948- 05/31/1969, south side Bank St PfcUSA: David E. Freestone 06/10/1948-08/27/1969, north side Colfax Ave Sp/5 USA: Harry Lee Theurkauf 05/15/1946- 06/12/1968, parking area S. Auburn St Capt USAF: Bruce Alan Jensen-11/18/1928 08/27/1967, east offramp W. Empire St 1st Lt USA: Ernest James Stidham: 03/14/1943-12/22/1968, northwest side of the Hwy 20 overpass of Mill St.

properties.name: Washington Brewery
properties.description: Founded 1858 by John Frank, the brewery operated until prohibition. The ownership remains in the Frank family to the present time. Note: The Frank family sold the building in 1973. It no longer functions as a brewery.

properties.name: Washington Elementary School
properties.description: 100 Years of uninterrupted use as a California public school. Having survived fires, floods, wars, time, and politics, this school has been doing its part to ensure the rights of American children to receive a basic education.

properties.name: Washington Hotel and Site
properties.description: Hessel Buisman built the first hotel on this site in 1857. It was destroyed by fire in August 1867, along with neighboring businesses. Buisman replaced it with a two-story hotel in that same year. In 1887, Buisman's daughter Harminia and her husband, E. T. Worthley, inherited the hotel, which burned in April 1896. The Worthleys reopened it with a third story in August 1896. John J. Rogers bought the hotel in 1910 for $50, and in 1911 he installed a water pipeline across the river to the Nice and Latta ditch to furnish water for the town. In 1912, he used the water to generate the first electricity for the hotel. Thomas B. Williamson, who had purchased a half interest in the hotel for $10 in 1911, bought out Rogers in 1916, and then sold it to Pietro Orzalli. Orzalli sold the property to R. F. Williamson (brother of Tom) in 1925. In 1926, Emory J. Haverstock bought the hotel and added dormer windows on the top floor, using lumber salvaged from the fire-ravaged Dugan Hotel (also in Washington). Haverstock sold it to Clark and Edna Waite for $10 in 1939. In 1955, the hotel was sold for $10 to Harold Moneyhun, former owner of the Holbrooke Hotel in Grass Valley, and Edwin Furano of Nevada City. In 1968, Thomas E. Walsh used siding salvaged from the National Hotel Annex in Nevada City to enclose the side porch on the upriver side of the hotel.

properties.name: Weiss-Hieronimus Brewery Cellar
properties.description: The Weiss-Hieronimus Brewery Cellar. Beginning in the early 1860s and continuing until Prohibition, the lower portion of this building was the storeroom for keg beer produced first by Emille (sic) Weiss for the Weiss Brewery then by Simon Hieronimus for the Hieronimus Brewery_later known as the Nevada Brewery. As the brewery business grew, Mr. Hieronimus established a bottling plant and stable on property across the street from this building. But keg beer continued to be stored here. A fire in the summer of 1896 destroyed the original house and severely damaged the cellar. But the Nevada Brewery emerged bigger and better than ever. It was at that time that the cellar was enlarged to its present size, a ventilation system installed and refrigeration added. In 2004 the property was acquired by Jesse and Kay Connor, who carefully restored the old brewery cellar and home. This plaque has been placed to honor the legacy of both the Weiss and Hieronimus families and to remind people that the best lager beer in Nevada County was once produced here. Note: Messrs Weiss and Hieronimus were each born in France. Weiss arrived in Nevada City in 1850 and immediately opened a brewery. Hieronimus arrived in Nevada City in 1876, then operated a brewery in North Bloomfield before taking over this brewery.

properties.name: Wells Fargo Building Site Nevada City
properties.description: Site of Old Wells Fargo Building 1853. Dedicated to the Pioneers of California by Hydraulic Parlor No. 56 N.S.G.W. July 5, 1926. Plaque erected by Wells Fargo Bank and Union Trust Co. S.F. Calif.

properties.name: Wells Fargo Building, North San Juan
properties.address: Corner of Flume St and Hwy 49
properties.description: Wells Fargo & Company 1854. Office and holding center for gold and currency, 1854-60. Block & Furth Co. Clothing Store, 1861-69. Chinese Laundry and store operated by Happy Go Lucky, 1890-1920. Bar and liquor store since 1934. First saloon owner, â€˜Ma' Cunningham, 1934.

properties.name: Western America Skisport Museum
properties.address: Exit on Boreal Ridge Rd on I-80 west of summit
properties.description: A monument and statue was placed by Snowshoe Thompson Lodge No. 78, Sons of Norway, Yuba City: Snowshoe Thompson. A hero of the Sierra Nevada mountains! Probably the first skier of the West. This Viking Son of Norway exemplified the spirit of a true pioneer: Strong, daring, faithful and courageous. He was the answer to CaliforniaÃ

properties.name: William Morris Stewart House
properties.description: William Morris Stewart Mansion Built 1855-56. Exact replica of wifeÃ

properties.name: Wolf Post Office
properties.description: The Nevada County Historical Landmarks Commission is pleased to announce the installation and dedication of a plaque honoring the Wolf Post Office on Sunday, October 6, 2019. Ranchers and farmers began to settle the historic agricultural community of Wolf in the 1860s. William Sweet arrived in Wolf in 1869, immigrating from Cornwall, and purchased 200 acres before his wife and children joined him in 1871. On August 24, 1888, a post office was established for the growing population of Wolf. John Sweet, one of Williamâ€™s sons, ran the post office from the living room of his ranch house and it soon became a community center and gathering place for approximately 75 residents of Wolf. It is believed that John Sweet was the longest serving U.S. Postmaster, continually running the Wolf Post Office for 52 years until his nephew W. B. Sweet took over on February 19, 1940. W. B. moved the Post Office from his uncleâ€™s home to a small building close by and served the Wolf community until 1956 when the post office was decommissioned with only 2 residents utilizing the service.

properties.name: Woman's Christian Temperance Union
properties.address: North side of Neal St between Mill and S. Church Sts, Grass Valley
properties.description: The first Woman's Christian Temperance Union in California organized in Grass Valley March 25, 1874. Rededicated and second plaque placed 2014: Originally placed near the corner of Neal and South Church Streets near the site of the old Congregational Church and the Women's Improvement Club building. Note: In 1972, the Congregational Church building was demolished to make way for a bank building. The first plaque was removed and stored in the nearby Methodist Church. In 2014, it was rededicated by the Manzanita Parlor of the Native Daughters at its present location on a monument constructed from petrified wood.

properties.name: World War I Memorial, Grass Valley
properties.address: Memorial Park, corner of Race St and Colfax Ave, Grass Valley
properties.description: Plaque dedicated 1921: Honor Roll. Heroes from the Grass Valley District who gave their lives for their country 1917-1918: Anthony J. Bevilacqua, Robert H. Beyer, Lawrence A. Brasher, Melvyn Frandy, Isaac Leslie Fleming, Hedley Hill, Lenville Dooley Jatunn, William Hague, William A. Kneebone, Albert Pinkham, William Hawley Moran, James Rodgers, Clarence Crase Thomas, J. M. Spencer, Elton Thomas Whiting, John Edward White. Recently, a small plaque at the base of the main plaque added U.S. Army Nurse Elina W. Hill to the Honor Roll. Note: The above inscription is on a very large monument. Surrounding it is a grove of 16 trees, each with a marker in front inscribed with the name of one of these men and the American Legion logotype.

properties.name: World War II & Korean War Memorial
properties.address: Memorial Park, corner of Race St and Colfax Ave, Grass Valley
properties.description: Plaque dedicated by Grass Valley Elks No. 538 and Frank Gallino Post No. 130 American Legion: World War II 1941-1946 Korean 1950-1955. To the men and women who made the supreme sacrifice for our country and her noble purpose, in memory of those who died that liberty might live. Note: Plaque is on a concrete monument that includes a flagpole and benches.

properties.name: You Bet Town Site
properties.address: Red Dog Rd, between Lowell Hill and Chalk Bluff Rds, six m. east of You Bet Rd exit on Hwy 174
properties.description: This mining camp, established between 1858-1860, consisted of 40 or 50 buildings by 1864. A school house was built midway between You Bet and Red Dog. The town was destroyed by fire in April 1869, partially rebuilt, and burned again in September 1873 and August 1915. The courts halted most hydraulic mining in 1884, but gold mining continued until the 1940s. The You Bet Post Office was closed in 1903.

properties.name: South Nevada County Chamber of Commerce
properties.description: The Chamber’s mission statement is to provide a voice for the business community and enhance the economic vitality of South Nevada County. properties.name: Booktown Books

We have added a new Rare Book Room where discriminating collectors can find fabulous rare, unusual and antiquarian books.

We are dedicated to building a better future through education, individual empowerment, community building and positive social change.

properties.name: Grass Valley Downtown Association
